name: Unit Counter System
description: A strategic combat relationship model where unit types, weapons, armor, abilities, or formations gain advantages and disadvantages against specific opponent categories.
design_notes: Counter systems create readable strategic adaptation when matchup rules are discoverable and consistent. The implementation should use structured counter_type_id and damage_modifier_table_id data rather than hidden special cases, with UI that explains why a unit wins or loses a matchup.
design_purpose: Encourage scouting, tech choices, mixed armies, and tactical positioning.
Prevent one unit type from solving every strategic problem.
Give designers explicit matchup data for balance tests and AI decision-making.
player_fantasy: The player feels clever when they identify an opponent's plan and field the right counter at the right moment.

parameters.name: unit_id
parameters.description: Unit or archetype participating in matchup evaluation.

parameters.name: counter_type_id
parameters.description: Strategic category used for counter rules.

parameters.name: damage_modifier_table_id
parameters.description: Data table mapping attacker and defender categories to modifiers.

parameters.name: target_entity_id
parameters.description: Target being evaluated for counter effects.

parameters.name: matchup_explanation_id
parameters.description: Player-facing reason shown in tooltips or combat preview.

parameters.name: tech_unlock_id
parameters.description: Optional research unlock that changes the matchup table.

parameters.name: balance_test_case_id
parameters.description: Scenario used by designers to verify matchup tuning.

edge_cases: A unit has multiple counter types and receives conflicting modifiers.
A tech unlock changes a matchup while projectiles or attacks are already in flight.
A shapeshifted, upgraded, or garrisoned unit should expose different counter categories.
A hidden counter modifier affects combat without being visible to the opponent or owner.
AI chooses a counter from outdated scouting information.
common_bugs: Damage modifiers apply twice because both weapon and target code read the same table.
Tooltip matchup text uses old data after balance patches.
Unit categories are localized strings and do not match damage_modifier_table_id keys.
Replay combat diverges because tech unlocks and attack resolution use different tick order.
AI evaluates counters using raw unit names instead of counter_type_id tags.
balancing_notes: Hard counters create clear decisions but can punish players who lack scouting or production flexibility.
Soft counters preserve unit identity while leaving room for upgrades, terrain, and micro decisions.
Every counter rule should have a readable exception policy for heroes, bosses, structures, or neutral units.
accessibility_notes: Show matchup explanations in text and icon form, not only through color-coded arrows.
Provide compact and expanded tooltip modes for high information density.
Keep combat previews readable for players who need more time to compare unit choices.

implementation_notes.unity: Store unit counter tags and damage_modifier_table_id in data assets, then resolve combat through one modifier pipeline. Tooltips, AI, and combat math should all read the same table and expose matchup_explanation_id for debugging.
implementation_notes.godot: Use Resources for units and counter tables, with stable counter_type_id keys. Combat code should evaluate structured tags, while UI preview queries the same resolver used by damage application.
implementation_notes.unreal: Gameplay Tags can represent counter types and tech unlocks. A combat component or subsystem should resolve damage modifiers authoritatively, replicate relevant results, and preserve tick order for replay.
implementation_notes.web: Keep matchup tables in versioned JSON and process combat in deterministic entity order. Use the same resolver for AI planning, UI previews, and final damage so browser and server simulations agree.

example_games.title: Age of Empires II
example_games.note: Uses unit classes and bonuses to create strategic counters.

example_games.title: StarCraft II
example_games.note: Uses unit matchups, upgrades, and scouting to drive counterplay.
